case_name: Lithium reduction from surface water for small drinking water system
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lithium-containing surface water intended for drinking water supply under low-energy and small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town draws drinking water from a river-influenced reservoir and has recently identified elevated lithium in source water during expanded monitoring for emerging contaminants. The utility needs a practical treatment approach that can be operated by a small staff and that avoids energy-intensive systems where possible. Conventional pretreatment for particles and seasonal algae is already familiar to the operator, but advanced brine handling capability is limited.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve meaningful lithium reduction for drinking water protection while maintaining reliable compliance with routine drinking water quality objectives.
secondary_goals: keep operation suitable for a small utility
avoid difficult residuals and concentrate management where possible

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited ability for brine or high-strength concentrate disposal; normal solids handling is available
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable year-round operation with limited operator attention is required

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter loading to protect downstream selective media
core_unit_functions.lithium_selective_ion_exchange_or_select: remove dissolved lithium using targeted exchange or adsorption media
core_unit_functions.cartridge_or_post_filtration: retain fines and polish treated water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety for drinking water distribution

applicability_rationale: lithium is the clearly defined primary dissolved contaminant and generally requires a selective dissolved-phase removal step rather than standard clarification alone
a selective ion-exchange or sorbent approach is more aligned with low-energy operation than pressure membrane systems
surface water solids and organics justify a simple pretreatment barrier to protect media performance
the treatment train is compact and more manageable for a small system than high-pressure desalination
potential_risks: lithium-selective media availability and replacement frequency must be verified at full scale
competing cations and changing water chemistry can reduce effective media capacity
spent regenerant or exhausted media still requires compliant handling

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: lower particulate and organic loading ahead of membranes
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: remove floc and reduce fouling potential
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide partial rejection of dissolved ions including lithium with lower pressure than reverse osmosis
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration can reduce dissolved contaminants while using less energy than reverse osmosis
pretreatment is appropriate for surface water to maintain membrane operability
this is a technically credible option when selective media performance is uncertain or not commercially available at needed scale
potential_risks: lithium rejection with nanofiltration may be incomplete compared with reverse osmosis and depends on membrane selection
concentrate disposal remains a significant issue for a small utility
membrane fouling control increases operator burden

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: prepare surface water for downstream membrane treatment by removing colloids
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove settled floc and reduce solids load
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: achieve strong dissolved ion rejection including lithium
core_unit_functions.stabilization: restore suitable finished water chemistry and corrosion control

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis is the most established high-rejection technology for dissolved salts and small monovalent ions when substantial lithium reduction is needed
the process is technically robust in terms of contaminant barrier performance
pretreatment train is standard practice for surface water membrane protection
potential_risks: high energy demand conflicts with the stated low-energy preference
concentrate management is difficult for small systems with limited disposal options
operator attention for scaling, fouling, and post-treatment chemistry is higher than preferred

core_unit_functions.conventional_pretreatment: control suspended solids and reduce fouling potential
core_unit_functions.electrodialysis_or_electrodialysis_rever: separate dissolved ions using an electric field across ion-exchange membranes
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish water and remove incidental particles

applicability_rationale: electromembrane processes are technically relevant for ionic contaminants such as lithium
in some brackish-water applications they can be alternatives to pressure membranes
the process is included as a viable but less straightforward option for a small drinking water utility
potential_risks: system complexity and specialized maintenance are often challenging for small utilities
brine stream management is still required
site-specific performance for lithium-dominant treatment goals may need piloting

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: remove suspended matter and some natural organic matter
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb hydrophobic organic contaminants and improve taste and odor

applicability_rationale: this train is familiar and practical for many surface-water systems
it can improve general drinking water quality and support conventional compliance objectives
however it is not a strong dissolved lithium removal approach and therefore ranks last
potential_risks: conventional clarification and GAC are not expected to provide reliable lithium reduction
selection of this train alone could fail the primary contaminant control objective
good operational simplicity does not compensate for poor lithium-specific performance

preferred_rank_pattern: targeted_lithium_selective_media_or_ion_exchange_with_simple_pretreatment should rank above membrane desalination under low-energy small-system conditions

must_reflect: small system preference for compact and supportable processes
low energy constraint should penalize high-pressure membrane options
limited brine handling should reduce the ranking of concentrate-producing technologies

must_contain: that lithium is a dissolved monovalent ion and is not effectively removed by standard clarification alone
why selective ion exchange or sorbent approaches fit the stated constraints better than reverse osmosis
why pretreatment is still useful for surface water even when the target contaminant is dissolved lithium

should_mention: uncertainty in lithium-selective media performance and replacement frequency
concentrate or brine disposal challenges for membrane-based options
possible interference from competing ions and changing source-water quality
